responses.questionText: How do you ensure shy students participate?
responses.optionText: Call them out randomly so they answer

allOptions.text: Call them out randomly so they answer
Pair them with confident students
Create low-pressure activities where everyone contributes
Let them stay quiet—they will talk when ready

responses.questionText: When was the last time you changed or experimented with a classroom strategy because you realised something wasn’t working?
responses.optionText: In the last 1–2 months

allOptions.text: This week or last week
In the last 1–2 months
Sometime this year
I usually stick to the strategies I already know

responses.questionText: How do you involve parents in the child’s learning?
responses.optionText: Share simple activities to do at home

allOptions.text: I don't involve them much
Ask them to ensure homework is done
Share simple activities to do at home
Work with them as partners, discussing progress and strategies

responses.questionText: Which activity do you prefer to build competencies?
responses.optionText: Only textbook questions

allOptions.text: Only textbook questions
Worksheets focused on recall
Real-life problem-solving tasks
Dictation and repetition

responses.questionText: Which statement do you agree with the MOST?
responses.optionText: Every child can learn, but some may need different types of support

allOptions.text: Some children are naturally better at learning than others
Every child can learn, but some may need different types of support
If students work hard, all students can reach the same level
Children who score well usually have higher ability

responses.questionText: Your class has finished a chapter, but many students can’t apply the concept to real-life problems. What do you do?
responses.optionText: Move ahead because syllabus completion is important

allOptions.text: Move ahead because syllabus completion is important
Give more revision worksheets for the same chapter
Pause and give new activities that involve using the concept in real situations
Ask students to memorise key points so they don’t forget

responses.questionText: What guides your lesson planning the most?
responses.optionText: Building specific competencies/skills

allOptions.text: Completing the chapter on time
Ensuring students remember key facts
Building specific competencies/skills
Following what other teachers do

responses.questionText: How do you view feedback on your teaching?
responses.optionText: It is uncomfortable

allOptions.text: It is uncomfortable
It is acceptable but not necessary
It helps me improve
I actively seek it

responses.questionText: How do you check if students can apply their learning?
responses.optionText: By asking them to memorise definitions

allOptions.text: By asking them to memorise definitions
By giving tasks that require real-life application
By giving more fill-in-the-blanks
By checking notebook completeness

responses.questionText: A student consistently makes the same mistake in Maths despite your explanations. What do you MOST likely do next?
responses.optionText: Ask the child to practice more questions of the same type

allOptions.text: Repeat the explanation one more time, but more clearly
Ask the child to practice more questions of the same type
Give a small task to check exactly where the misconception is happening
Tell the parent the child needs more practice at home

responses.questionText: How often do you communicate learning progress to parents (not just marks)?
responses.optionText: Only during PTMs

allOptions.text: Only during PTMs
Once every few months
Monthly updates
Regular short updates (weekly/fortnightly)

responses.questionText: How do you stay updated about best practices?
responses.optionText: I follow what senior teachers do

allOptions.text: I don’t actively update myself
I follow what senior teachers do
I attend trainings when assigned
I proactively learn from courses, reading, or peers

responses.questionText: When two students learn at very different speeds, what do you usually do?
responses.optionText: Ask the faster learner to help the slower one

allOptions.text: Continue teaching at the same pace for everyone
Give extra homework to the slower learner
Provide varied support so both can progress
Ask the faster learner to help the slower one

responses.questionText: When a child performs poorly, how do you find the cause?
responses.optionText: Assume the child didn’t study

allOptions.text: Assume the child didn’t study
Re-check the question paper
Use small diagnostic tasks to find exact gaps
Talk to the parent first

responses.questionText: How often do you assess progress during teaching?
responses.optionText: Only when I feel students are slow

allOptions.text: Only during exams
Only when I feel students are slow
Weekly informal checks
Daily quick checks (exit slips, oral checks, tasks)

responses.questionText: When did you last try a new teaching method or tool?
responses.optionText: Within the last year

allOptions.text: I rarely try new methods
Within the last year
Within the last few months
This week or last week

responses.questionText: When parents ask what they can do to support learning at home, you MOST often:
responses.optionText: Give them 1–2 simple activities they can do with the child

allOptions.text: Give them 1–2 simple activities they can do with the child
Tell them to ensure the child completes homework on time
Say that most learning happens in school and not to worry
Usually do not give suggestions; parents choose their own methods

responses.questionText: When students don’t understand a topic, what do you usually think first?
responses.optionText: I need to explain it more clearly

allOptions.text: I need to explain it more clearly
Which students are struggling and why
How many students answered correctly
What activity might help them grasp it

responses.questionText: Before starting a new chapter, how do you check learning levels?
responses.optionText: I don’t—everyone starts fresh

allOptions.text: I don’t—everyone starts fresh
By giving a quick informal check (questions/activity)
By checking the previous exam scores
By asking students if they already know the topic

responses.questionText: What do you usually discuss with parents?
responses.optionText: Mostly marks and homework

allOptions.text: Only if child is misbehaving
Mostly marks and homework
Strengths and areas for improvement
Specific strategies to support learning at home

responses.questionText: When a lesson doesn’t work as expected, you are most likely to:
responses.optionText: Discuss it with another teacher

allOptions.text: Reflect on what you could change next time
Discuss it with another teacher
Stick to the plan and move ahead
Check student work to see what went wrong

responses.questionText: How do you understand a child’s strengths or interests?
responses.optionText: By looking at test scores

allOptions.text: By looking at test scores
By observing classroom behaviour and interactions
By asking parents during PTMs
I don’t specifically track strengths or interests
